coredata.dc:title: Long-wavelength emissive solid-state carbon dots in nanoporous glass with excellent thermal stability
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yunxiu
Zhang, Ning
Yang, Luyun
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent solid-state carbon dots have drawn significant attention because of their intriguing optical properties, e.g., broad absorption, stable fluorescence, etc. However, obtaining the fluorescent solid-state carbon dots with a long-wavelength emission is still a challenging problem due to the notorious aggregation-induced quenching problem. In this paper, a technically feasible and low-cost approach for preparing fluorescent solid-state carbon dots is presented. The carbon dots are encapsulated in the nanoporous glass using the solution-phase-doping method. The size of nanoporous glass is tuned to 4.95 nm by regulating the ratio of boron to silicon to match the prepared CDs. Owing to the physical isolation and chemical connection of the -C-O-Si functional groups, the CDs are dispersed uniformly into the nanochannels of nanoporous glass. In this way, the aggregation-induced quenching problem of carbon dots is solved. The experiment results show that the photoluminescence has the excitation-independent emission at 609 nm, i.e., the prepared solid-state carbon dots are with orange fluorescence. More importantly, the solid-state carbon dots preserve orange fluorescence after heating treatment (400 °C) in the ambient atmosphere.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanoporous glass
Solid-state photoluminescence
Long-wavelength emission
Thermal stability

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the fund of the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (NO.2019M662598). The authors thank the Nanocarbon-HUST group for the assistance in the experimental facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Ashby map of the solid-state CDs with different temperatures and emission wavelengths.

(a) The schematic diagram of the progress from pristine CDs to CDs-in-NPG, (b) The UV–vis absorption spectrum and PL spectra of pristine CDs, (c) The UV–vis absorption spectrum and PL spectra of pristine CDs-in-NPG.
The time-resolved luminescence decay curves of pristine CDs and CDs-in-NPG.

(a) TEM image of pristine CDs, the inset is the HRTEM image, (b) AFM image of pristine CDs, the inset figure reveals the height of CDs, (c) FESEM image of NPG, (d) pore size distribution of NPG, (e) TEM image of CDs-in-NPG, (f) EELS elemental mapping of the CDs-in-NPG.

(a) XPS full spectra of pristine CDs and CDs-in-NPG, (b) FTIR spectra of pristine CDs and CDs-in-NPG, (c) internal process of the reaction between encapsulated CDs and NPG, (d) the schematic of structure and energy level alignments of CDs and CDs-in-NPG, HOMO, highest occupied molecular orbital; LUMO, lowest unoccupied molecular orbital.

(a) The PL spectra of CDs-in-NPG with different temperatures from 25 °C to 400 °C, (b) relative PL intensity of CDs-in-NPG versus temperature range from 25 °C to 400 °C, the insets are the photos of CDs-in-NPG.

head.ce:title: Long-wavelength emissive solid-state carbon dots in nanoporous glass with excellent thermal stability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Material Processing & Die and Mould Technology, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Material Processing & Die and Mould Technology
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Material Processing & Die and Mould Technology, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, 430074, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, 430074, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent solid-state carbon dots have drawn significant attention because of their intriguing optical properties, e.g., broad absorption, stable fluorescence, etc. However, obtaining the fluorescent solid-state carbon dots with a long-wavelength emission is still a challenging problem due to the notorious aggregation-induced quenching problem. In this paper, a technically feasible and low-cost approach for preparing fluorescent solid-state carbon dots is presented. The carbon dots are encapsulated in the nanoporous glass using the solution-phase-doping method. The size of nanoporous glass is tuned to 4.95 nm by regulating the ratio of boron to silicon to match the prepared CDs. Owing to the physical isolation and chemical connection of the -C-O-Si functional groups, the CDs are dispersed uniformly into the nanochannels of nanoporous glass. In this way, the aggregation-induced quenching problem of carbon dots is solved. The experiment results show that the photoluminescence has the excitation-independent emission at 609 nm, i.e., the prepared solid-state carbon dots are with orange fluorescence. More importantly, the solid-state carbon dots preserve orange fluorescence after heating treatment (400 °C) in the ambient atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:para:
Carbon dots (CDs) is one of the promising fluorescent nanomaterials because of the broad absorption, stable fluorescence, facile surface modification, good biocompatibility, and eco-friendly preparation, etc. [1–4]. Thus, CDs have been attracting attention in many fields, e.g., sensing[5,6], biological imaging[7,8], optoelectronic devices[9], photocatalysis[10], and luminescence [11,12]. However, the solid-state CDs yield degenerated photoluminescence (PL) intensity since the aggregation between CDs results in the resonance energy transfer (RET),
π
-
π
stacking interaction, and surface charge [13]. In other words, the aggregation between solid-state CDs leads to the aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) problem. Furthermore, their unknown thermal stability limits their potential applications[8]. Therefore, it is a great challenge to achieve solid-state CDs that exhibit strong emission together with excellent thermal stability.

To address this issue, embedding CDs in the solid-state matrix materials has been developed to effectively resistant the AIQ effect. The matrix materials include i). polymer matrices[14,15], ii). organic matrices, e.g., starch [16], iii). inorganic matrices, e.g., montmorillonite[17], silica[18–20], BaSO4 [21], NaCl[22], ZrO2 [23], and iv) layered matrices, e.g. boron nitride nanosheets(BNNSs)[24], layered double hydroxides(LDHs)[25].

Since the physical dispersion and formed functional groups on the surfaces of the matrices, the aggregation of CDs is inhibited[26–29]. In this way, the AIQ problem is eased and the PL intensities are enhanced. However, the matrices can damage under high temperature, resulting in a dramatic decrease of the PL intensities[17,24]. Thus, it limits the potential applications of solid-state CDs for high-power optoelectronic devices and laser systems.

Thanks to the good physical properties and excellent thermal stability of silica matrices, the prepared solid-state CDs based on silica matrices can withstand high temperatures. Y. Zhan et al. reported the yellow-emitting silica/CDs composites that can withstand 100 °C [18]. J. Wang et al. developed the yellow-emitting CDs/silica film that can be heated to 150 °C [19]. Besides, Q. Chang et al. prepared the blue-emitting solid-state CDs based on silica matrix (SBA-15), the PL intensity can retain 95% at the temperature of 300 °C [20]. However, the PL wavelengths of solid-state CDs based on the above materials are most in the blue-yellow emission region.

CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method. The p-Phenylenediamine (pPD) and anhydrous aluminum chloride (AlCl3) were used as the raw materials and the mole ratio was 5:3. The mixture of 0.5 mmol pPD with 0.3 mmol AlCl3 was transferred to Teflon autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. Then, the samples were cooled to room temperature. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min, and then further purified via a 0.22 mm filter membrane. Then the solution was subjected to dialysis in order to remove the excess precursor and side products. Then the as-prepared CDs were recovered as powders through a freeze-drying process for further characterization and use.

The preparation of NPG mainly includes three steps: first, the sodium borosilicate (SBS) glass was prepared. The high-purity raw materials of 64(wt%) SiO2-28(wt%) H3BO3-6(wt%) Na2CO3 were fused at the temperature of 1550 °C for 3 h to obtain the SBS bulk glass with a size of 200 mm × 200 mm × 100 mm. Then the SBS bulk glass was mechanically cut into 20 mm × 20 mm × 2 mm small sheets. Second, the SBS glass sheets were treated by phase separation technology. The SBS glass sheets were placed in the phase-separation furnace and heat-treated at the temperature of 600 °C for 24 h. After the phase-separation process, the SBS glass sheets were separated into two interconnected phases of boron-rich phase and silicon-rich phase. Finally, the SBS glass sheets were leached in hydrochloric acid solutions at the temperature of 80 °C for 36 h, where the boron-rich phase in SBS glass sheets was dissolved. The hydrochloric acid is at a concentration of 1 M. After washing with deionized water 3 times and dried at the temperature of 600 °C (increase the temperature at the rate of 1.5 °C/min), the purified silica skeleton with interconnected and uniformly distributed nanopores and nanochannels was obtained and labeled as NPG. The preparing progress of nanoporous glass (NPG) is illustrated in Fig. S1.

The CDs-in-NPG are prepared as follows. The synthesized CDs were dissolved in anhydrous ethanol to obtain CDs solution with a concentration of 0.049 mg/mL. Then, the NPG sheet was soaked in CDs solution for 3 days, the composite sample was taken out and naturally dried in a clean environment. The solid-state composite of NPG sheets and CDs was obtained and labeled as CDs-in-NPG.

The experimental materials are shown as follows. p-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2, analytically pure), anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH, analytically pure), and anhydrous aluminum chloride (AlCl3, analytically pure) were purchased from Wuhan Reagent Company. All the chemicals are directly used without further purification. The deionized water used in the experiment was prepared through the water circulation system. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken using the Talos F200x. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were taken using the FESEM Sirion 200. Fourier Transform Infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on the VERTEX 70. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded using the AxIS-ULTRA DLD-600 W spectrometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by the FP-6500 fluorescence spectrometer of Jasco (Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on the Solidspec-3700. All the tests were carried out at room temperature.

The photoluminescence (PL) properties (including the UV–vis absorption spectra, PL spectra, and PL lifetime) of the CDs-in-NPG are explored in the experiments. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the prepared pristine CDs in this work are the blue emission. The UV–vis absorption spectrum and PL spectra of pristine CDs are shown in Fig. 2(b). In the UV region, a single peak is observed at 244 nm, corresponding to the
π
-
π
* transitions of C = C and C = N bonds, respectively, in the aromatic rings, which do not typically generate fluorescence [35]. But, in the lower-energy region, the spectrum exhibits distinct absorption bands at 301 nm and 533 nm, contributing to the PL of the pristine CDs in the blue light region or long-wavelength region. From the PL spectra, the pristine CDs show the excitation-independent emission at 394 nm when increasing the excitation wavelength from 260 nm to 380 nm, which ascribes to the N related functional groups[36]. Besides, there is very weak emission around 640 nm when the excitation ranges from 400 nm to 540 nm. However, the PL of the pristine CDs is quenched when the excitation wavelength is at 560 nm. Unlikely the pristine CDs, the CDs-in-NPG are obtained by encapsulated into the 3D nanostructure of NPG (Fig. 2(a)), thus, the UV–vis spectrum and PL spectra are different the pristine CDs, as shown in Fig. 2 (c). The absorption bands are mainly at 202 nm, 274 nm, and 520 nm, respectively, which differ from that of the pristine CDs, indicating the changes of the surface states. From the PL spectra, the CDs-in-NPG have the excitation-independent emission wavelengths at 609 nm when the excitation wavelengths range from 260 nm to 560 nm. Even some reported CDs are accompanied by the well-known excitation-dependent emission, which is attributed to the surface state emission from different functional groups [37–39]. The excitation-independent emission of CDs-in-NPG is related to the particle size formed in the nanopores of NPG and formed functional groups of -C-O-Si. The above properties are attributed to the new energy bandgap[40,36], thus excitation-independent emission is obtained here. Besides, the absorption spectrum of NPG is shown in Fig. S3. It can be seen that the absorption peak at 217 nm is from the absorption of silica materials. But there is no absorption when the wavelength is over 300 nm. Thus, the PL of the CDs-in-NPG is originated from CDs encapsulated into the NPG. Moreover, CDs-in-NPG yield the highest intensity with the excitation wavelength at 560 nm. It is noted that the PL intensity of the pristine CDs is much lower than that of the CDs-in-NPG, which is caused by the AIQ of pristine CDs in the solid-state. Because of the NPG effects, the CDs-in-NPG don’t show the PL quenching in the long-wavelength region and PL intensities have been enhanced greatly. For example, the PL intensity has increased to 91.7 times when the excitation wavelength is at 540 nm.

Fig. 3 shows the time-resolved luminescence decay curves of pristine CDs and CDs-in-NPG. The average PL lifetimes of the prepared CDs-in-NPG and the pristine CDs are 1.67 ns and 1.55 ns, respectively. The process is exhibited in Table S1 and Table S2, respectively. It can be seen that they can be fitted by a tri- or dual-exponential formula, involving two short-lived components of
τ
1
,
τ
2
and a long-lived component
τ
3
for CDs-in-NPG, yet only short-lived components
τ
1
,
τ
2
for pristine CDs. A long-lived component
τ
3
for the CDs-in-NPG, which are assigned to the recombination of core states and surface states [41]. Since the featured pore channels of NPG can alter both the luminescent centers and surface states of pristine CDs, encapsulating CDs in the NPG improves the average PL lifetime by a significant margin (more than 10%).

The experiments show that encapsulating CDs in NPG improves the PL properties in the following aspects: i) achieving long-wavelength emission at 609 nm (CDs emission are at 394 nm) and enhanced PL intensity by 91.7 times at the same excitation wavelength, and ii) the PL lifetime is elevated more than 10%.

To further characterize the morphology and structure of the pristine CDs, NPG, and CDs-in-NPG, the morphologies and the heights of the pristine CDs are measured by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and tapping-mode atomic force (AFM) microscopy, shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b), respectively. The TEM image shows that the pristine CDs are uniform in size (an average of
≈
5 nm). The high-resolution image in the inset of Fig. 4 (a) displays a lattice fringe of 0.26 nm, indicating the graphite-like structure of the crystalline core of pristine CDs. The AFM image shows that the height of the prepared CDs is close to 5 nm, too. The results show that the average size of the pristine CDs is 4
~
5 nm.

Fig. 4 (c) shows the FESEM image of NPG. It reveals that the NPG has interconnected and uniformly distributed nanoscale pores. Nitrogen sorption analysis is performed to obtain detailed insight into the porosity of the NPG using an ASAP 2020 M (Micromeritics, USA) automated surface area and pore size analyzer. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the NPG can be calculated by typical nitrogen (77 K) adsorption–desorption isotherms according to the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) model [32]. As shown in Fig. S4, the NPG exhibits nitrogen (77 K) adsorption–desorption isothermal similar to IUPAC type IV [42]. Calculating based on the measured isotherms, the BET specific surface area of the NPG is calculated to be 129.1301 m2/g. Such a large surface area primarily arises from the interconnected and uniformly distributed nanoscale pores, which is conducive to the adsorption of doped CDs. The pore size distribution in NPG is plotted based on nitrogen-adsorption–desorption isotherms and Barrett-Joyner-halenda (BJH) method, as shown in Fig. 4 (d). Most of the pores have diameters around 4.95 nm. The full width at the half maximum (FWHM) of the distribution is narrow (0.62 nm). It indicates that the nanopores in the prepared NPG have superb homogeneity. Fig. 4 (e) and (f) are the TEM and Electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) images of CDs-in-NPG, respectively. Because of the match between sizes of CDs and pore diameters of NPG, the uniformly combined structure of CDs-in-NPG is obtained, as shown in Fig. 4 (e). From the TEM image of CDs-in-NPG, the connected CDs are observed in the nanoporous skeleton. Meanwhile, the EELS image of C and Si elements verifies the uniformity and combination, indicating the CDs are uniformly dispersed in the NPG.

Based on the results shown in Fig. 4, it is verified that the diameters of the pores of the NPG are well-matched with the average size of the pristine CDs. In this way, the solid-state CDs can be homogeneously encapsulated in the NPG.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra are conducted to analyze the elemental composition and bonding configurations of the pristine CDs and CDs-in-NPG. Fig. 5 (a) shows the full XPS spectra of pristine CDs and CDs-in-NPG. Compared to the pristine CDs, the prepared CDs-in-NPG have two additional peaks of
Si
2 p
and
Si
2 s
, and the content of C and N decreases significantly. More importantly, a significant decrease in the nitrogen-to-carbon ratio (from 20.9 % to 0.1 %) is observed. It indicates that the functional groups are formed between CDs and NPG, i.e., chemical bonds facilitate the dispersion of the CDs on the pore channels of NPG. The high-resolution XPS spectra of pristine CDs and CDs-in-NPG are shown in Fig. S2(a), the
C
1 s
high-resolution XPS spectrum confirms the existence of C-C/C = C (285 eV), C-N/C = O (286.1 eV), and C-N/C = O (287.9 eV). It should be noted that the C = C area percentage in CDs-in-NPG increases up to 70.84% comparing to that in pristine CDs (5.78%), indicating the functional groups in CDs-in-NPG changed after encapsulating CDs into the structure of NPG. Moreover, the
Si
2 p
XPS spectrum of CDs-in-NPG (Fig. S2(b)) exhibits two main peaks at 101.80 eV and 102.40 eV, corresponding to Si-O-C and Si-C, respectively [43].It means that the new functional groups have been formed in CDs-in-NPG.

The surface functional groups of pristine CDs and CDs-in-NPG are investigated by Fourier Transform Infrared spectra (FTIR), shown in Fig. 5 (b). Many bonds were observed in the pristine CDs, such as C = O at 1577 cm−1, C = N at 1618 cm−1 and C-O at 1132 cm−1, N-H and O-H at 3500 cm−1-3200 cm−1 and C = C at 1472 cm−1. C-N stretching vibration at 1349 cm−1 indicates the form of N-containing heterocyclic rings inside the CDs. It is revealed that the CDs are composed of aromatic structures and possessed abundant -NH2 on their surface [44,45]. Besides, the peaks of -NH2 can not be observed in the FTIR spectrum due to the overlap between -NH2 and -OH [46]. It is revealed that the CDs are composed of aromatic structures and possessed abundant -NH2 on their surface. Compared to the pristine CDs, CDs-in-NPG exhibit the stretching vibration bands at 927 cm−1 and 1148 cm−1 corresponding to the Si-O-H and Si-O-C. [47]. The O-Si-O symmetric bending vibration at 400–600 cm−1. The stretching vibration bands at 1090 cm−1 and 3481 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of Si-O-Si and O-H, respectively [48,49], suggesting that interactions occurred between confined CDs and the internal surface of NPG. Meanwhile, the stretching vibrations peaks at 1501 cm−1 and 1472 cm−1 are omitted, i.e., functional groups of Si-O-H and Si-O-C are formed and the amount of C-N, and C = O functional groups decrease. It indicates that the new functional groups of -C-O-Si are formed by a reaction between -NH2 of pristine CDs and -Si-OH of NPG, as illustrated in Fig. 5 (c).

The results of characterization of CDs-in-NPG show that the CDs are homogeneously dispersed in the pore channels of NPG based on two mechanisms including i) physical isolation using the featured nano-scale structure of NPG, and ii) chemical bond-facilitated dispersion using the formed functional groups of -C-O-Si. In this way, the aggregation of solid-state CDs is countered and the AIQ problem can be eased.

Based on the above results from PL spectra, absorption, SEM images, XPS spectra, and FTIR spectra, orange emission as well as the increased PL lifetime for CDs-in-NPG are due to the interaction between confined CDs and the inner surface of NPG that altered the luminescent centers and surface states of CDs, thus resulting in the changed optical properties. A schematic energy level structure of CDs before and after encapsulation inside the channels of NPG is proposed in Fig. 5 (d).

Comparing to the blue-emitting pristine CDs, the orange emission of the CDs-in-NPG can be attributed to the dispersion of the nanostructure of NPG. The introduction of the nanostructure of NPG changes the surface states and sp2 fraction of CDs.

As we know that, the emission wavelength of PL materials depends on the energy gap between excited states and the ground state [8]. The new functional groups of -C-O-Si introduces new energy levels to the CDs-in-NPG, resulting in the reduction of the energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). The decreased energy gap leads to the long-wavelength emitting (at 609 nm) of CDs-in-NPG. Besides, as reported by Chien et al. [50], the sp2 fraction can be defined as the peak area ratios of sp2 bonded (C = C) carbon atoms calculated from XPS results. Compared to the pristine CDs, the sp2 fraction of CDs-in-NPG has a significant increase (from 5.78% to 70.84%) after encapsulating CDs into NPG. With consistency to previous works on PL mechanisms of CDs[51–54], the increased sp2 fraction also contributed to the orange-emission of CDs-in-NPG. Therefore, the synergistic effect of functional group and sp2 fraction leads to the orange-emission of CDs-in-NPG. At the same time, the combination of CDs and NPG quenched the blue emission from some organic groups on CDs surface, as shown in Fig. 5 (d). Moreover, the CDs are dispersed into the 3D nanostructure of the NPG, the AIQ effect is inhibited. Thus, the PL intensity of CDs-in-NPG has been enhanced.

Thermal quenching stability of phosphor is of particular importance for their practical applicability. Experiments are conducted to evaluate the thermal stability of CDs-in-NPG. In the experiments, CDs-in-NPG were heated at 100 °C, 200 °C, 300 °C, and 400 °C for 6 h, respectively. Then, the samples were cooled to room temperature. At the optimal excitation wavelengths, the spectra of CDs-in-NPG with different heating treatment temperatures are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The PL wavelengths are all in the orange emission region (over 600 nm). The emission wavelengths shift 5 nm (from 609 to 614 nm) while the heating treatment temperatures went from 25 to 400 °C.

Furthermore, the relative PL intensities (I/I0) are calculated to evaluate the thermal stability of CDs-in-NPG. The baseline is the PL intensity of CDs-in-NPG in room temperature, named as I0. The results show that the variation of PL intensities is within 10% while the heating treatment temperatures range from 25 to 300 °C, shown in Fig. 6 (b). Moreover, the samples of CDs-in-NPG are not broken and keep well after the heating treatment, as shown in the inset of Fig. 6 (b). It indicates that the prepared CDs-in-NPG possess satisfactory thermal stability. It can be attributed to the fact that NPG can retain the uniform nanopores and nanochannels at very high temperatures, which is elaborated in our previous work [55].

In this work, CDs are encapsulated inside the nanoporous space of NPG successfully via the solution-phase-doping method. The unique nanoporous microenvironment of NPG with a pore size of 4.95 nm causes the interaction between the CDs and NPG, where the new functional groups -C-O-Si have been formed. It effectively inhibited the aggregation of encapsulated CDs inside the NPG structure. As a consequence, solid-state CDs-in-NPG exhibit orange fluorescence with enhanced PL intensity of 91.7 times in contrast to pristine CDs at the same excitation wavelength. Importantly, solid-state CDs-in-NPG still emit strong orange fluorescence even after heating treatment at 400 °C in the ambient atmosphere. This work provides a new way to prepare the solid-state fluorescence via nanoporous structure glass. The methods and analysis can also be used to extend the preparation of the long-wavelength emission and thermal stable CDs. The strong orange emitting CDs with high thermal stability that are prepared in this manner, having great importance for the further implementation as an active medium in luminescent optoelectronic devices and laser systems.

Yunxiu Ma worked on the conceptualization, experiment plan, data processing and writing. Ning Zhang assisted with the implementation of the experimental plan. Luyun Yang worked on the supervision, review and editing. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the fund of the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (NO.2019M662598). The authors thank the Nanocarbon-HUST group for the assistance in the experimental facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.04.060.
